Route builder — order, dates, book

Pick your start date, tick places and set nights for each — check-in and check-out dates are computed by chaining from the start date. Edit the # number to reorder: type 2 to slot a place into position 2, everything after shifts down and keeps its nights. The trip always ends back at Krakow. The trip map above redraws your route as you go.

#PlaceNightsCheck-inCheck-outKmHWeatherBook

Km are driving distances between consecutive stops; "~" legs are estimates. Hours assume ~95 km/h trip average incl. short breaks. Rows marked alternative are researched options near the route — hover for why, tick to swap them in. Weather is the stay's high/low from Open-Meteo; "≈" means last year's same dates (too far out for a forecast). Booking/Airbnb links open pre-filled searches — availability and prices are theirs.

The places — profiles

Every stop in one gallery. Places on your route in the builder above float to the top, numbered in visit order. Ticket links go to official operators.

Tarnów

stop · PL

Discover Poland's warmest city, boasting a pristine Renaissance old town, rich Jewish heritage, and easy access to sandy lakeside beaches and pine-forested hiking trails.

Stay: €50-90/n boutique hotels near Rynek — budget apartments €35-50/n

Try: Tarninówka (sloe berry liqueur), Galician beef broth, pierogi

  • Tarnów Town Hall & Regional Museum ~€4 · Closed on Mondays; climb the tower for panoramic views of the Renaissance square.
  • Ethnographic Museum (Romani Exhibition) ~€3 · Features Europe's first permanent exhibition dedicated to Romani history and culture.
  • Where to eat
  • Pół na Pół — Excellent Neapolitan-style pizzas, homemade pasta, and premium steaks in a stylish, modern interior. ~€15-25/pp.
  • La Scala — Authentic Italian dining featuring fresh seafood dishes, classic risottos, and a curated Italian wine list. ~€20-30/pp.
  • In & around
  • Radłów Lake (Riviera Radłowska) — 20 min drive, featuring sandy beaches, pine forests, and clear waters perfect for swimming.
  • Ciężkowice Petrified City — 40 min drive, scenic hiking trails winding through bizarre sandstone rock formations and pine-oak woods.
  • Zalipie Painted Village — 40 min drive, world-famous village where houses, barns, and bridges are covered in vibrant hand-painted floral motifs.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • The city center has a paid parking zone active on weekdays; pay at the physical meters or use mobile apps like MoBILET.
  • Pack strong insect repellent if you plan to hike in Ciężkowice or relax by the sandy beaches of Radłów Lake, as mosquitoes are common.
  • Many local museums and art galleries are closed on Mondays, so plan your cultural itinerary for Tuesday through Sunday.
  • Interesting facts
  • Tarnów is scientifically documented as the warmest city in Poland, holding the record for the longest thermal summer.
  • The town is nicknamed the 'Pearl of the Polish Renaissance' due to its intact 16th-century architectural layout and magnificent Town Hall.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Krynica-Zdrój

stop · PL

A historic spa town nestled in the Beskid Sądecki mountains, Krynica-Zdrój offers lush pine forests, scenic hiking trails, and a vibrant cultural scene dedicated to naive artist Nikifor.

Stay: €60-110/n town center — cheaper guesthouses on the outskirts €40-60/n

Try: Zuber mineral water, Oscypek (grilled sheep cheese with cranberry), Krynica gingerbread

  • Słotwiny Arena Treetop Path ~€18 · Combined tickets with the lift are available; great for panoramic pine forest views.
  • Jaworzyna Krynicka Gondola ~€15 · Buy online to skip ticket window queues on sunny weekends.
  • Nikifor Museum ~€4 · Housed in the wooden Romanówka villa; closed on Mondays.
  • Where to eat
  • Restauracja Dwóch Świętych — Premium local steaks and refined European dishes in an elegant setting, ~€25-35/pp.
  • Restauracja Włoska — Authentic wood-fired pizzas, seafood pasta, and Italian classics, ~€15-25/pp.
  • In & around
  • Klimkówka Lake — 40 min drive, offering a sandy and pebble beach, windsurfing, and pine-fringed shores.
  • Muszyna — 15 min drive, featuring sensory gardens, castle ruins, and mineral water tasting.
  • Tylicz — 10 min drive, home to a historic wooden church and natural carbon dioxide mofettes.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in the town center is strictly regulated with paid parking zones; keep coins handy or use mobile parking apps.
  • The famous Zuber mineral water has an incredibly strong, sulfurous taste—buy a small cup before committing to a full bottle.
  • Mountain weather changes quickly; pack layers and waterproof gear even for short hikes in the Beskid Sądecki.
  • Interesting facts
  • Krynica-Zdrój is the birthplace of Nikifor Krynicki, one of the world's most celebrated naive painters.
  • The town earned the moniker 'Pearl of Polish Spas' due to its abundance of natural therapeutic mineral springs discovered in the 18th century.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Sandomierz

stop · PL

Perched on a high bluff overlooking the Vistula River, Sandomierz captivates with its perfectly preserved medieval old town, mysterious underground tunnels, and nearby ancient loess ravines.

Stay: €60-100/n old town — cheaper in suburbs €40-60

Try: Sandomierski apple cider, regional wines from local vineyards, pierogi with local cheese

  • Underground Tourist Route (Podziemna Trasa Turystyczna) ~€6 · Guided tours only; the underground temperature is a constant 12°C, so bring a light jacket.
  • Sandomierz Royal Castle (Muzeum Zamkowe) ~€5 · Free admission on Wednesdays for the permanent exhibitions.
  • Opatów Gate (Brama Opatowska) Viewing Terrace ~€3 · Requires climbing narrow, steep wooden stairs to reach the top panoramic platform.
  • Where to eat
  • Restauracja Widnokrąg — Upscale dining near the Opatów Gate serving modern Polish-European cuisine. Signature: Premium beef steak with local demi-glace and regional wine pairings. ~€25-35/pp.
  • Restauracja Pod Ciżemką — Set in a historic 16th-century townhouse cellar. Signature: Roasted trout with herbs and traditional Polish wild mushroom soup. ~€20-30/pp.
  • Pizzeria i Restauracja Mariano — A cozy, highly-rated Italian spot. Signature: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza and seafood linguine. ~€15-25/pp.
  • In & around
  • Jezioro Tarnobrzeskie — 20 min drive, featuring expansive sandy beaches, pine-fringed shores, and water sports.
  • Góry Pieprzowe (Pepper Mountains) — 10 min drive, offering scenic hiking trails through 500-million-year-old shale rocks and wild rose reserves.
  • Krzyżtopór Castle in Ujazd — 40 min drive, spectacular ruins of a massive 17th-century fortress-palace built on a calendar-based design.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • The historic Old Town is mostly pedestrianized; park your car in the designated paid lots just outside the medieval walls near the castle.
  • Wear sturdy walking shoes with good grip, as the town's steep cobblestone streets and the nearby Queen Jadwiga Ravine can be very slippery.
  • Carry some Polish Złoty (PLN) cash, as smaller local wine cellars and souvenir stalls in the ravines do not always accept cards.
  • Interesting facts
  • Sandomierz is built over a labyrinth of merchant cellars and tunnels dug into the soft loess soil during the Middle Ages.
  • The surrounding Sandomierz Basin is one of Poland's warmest regions, making it a premier hub for vineyards and apple orchards.
  • The town is widely famous across Poland as the scenic setting for 'Ojciec Mateusz', a long-running TV series about a crime-solving priest.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Kazimierz Dolny

stop · PL

A picturesque artistic enclave on the Vistula River, where Renaissance architecture meets sandy riverbanks, pine-forested gorges, and vibrant art galleries.

Stay: €60-110/n boutique guesthouse near Rynek — cheaper guesthouses €40-70/n

Try: Kogut kazimierski (rooster-shaped pastry), local smoked Vistula plum brandy, fresh river trout

  • Castle Ruins and Watchtower ~€3.50 · Ticket includes access to both the lower castle ruins and the medieval watchtower.
  • Three Crosses Hill (Góra Trzech Krzyży) ~€1.50 · Cash only at the entry booth; offers the best panoramic view of the town and Vistula river.
  • Celej House Museum (Kamienica Celejowska) ~€4.00 · Showcases the town's rich history as an art colony; closed on Tuesdays.
  • Where to eat
  • Trattoria Koszary — Authentic wood-fired Italian pizzas, fresh handmade pastas, and excellent steaks in a stylishly restored military barracks. ~€20-30/pp
  • Zielona Tawerna — Charming, artistic restaurant serving excellent local fish, seafood dishes, and traditional Polish cuisine in a cozy garden setting. ~€25-35/pp
  • In & around
  • Root Gorge (Korzeniowy Dół) — 5 min drive, a breathtaking loess gorge with towering, exposed tree roots perfect for hiking.
  • Janowiec Castle — 45 min drive (or a scenic ferry crossing), massive 16th-century castle ruins overlooking the Vistula valley.
  • Albrechtówka Viewpoint & Mięćmierz — 10 min drive, an old rafters' village with thatched huts, leading to a pine-forested cliff overlook.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • The historic center is mostly pedestrianized; use the large paid parking lots along the Vistula riverbank to avoid fines.
  • Carry cash (Polish Zloty) as many small art galleries, market stalls, and the Three Crosses Hill entrance do not accept cards.
  • Beat the heavy weekend crowds from Warsaw by exploring the famous loess gorges and hiking trails early in the morning.
  • Interesting facts
  • The town's famous rooster-shaped pastry (Kogut) is tied to a local legend of a clever rooster who saved the town by outsmarting the devil.
  • Kazimierz Dolny has been a thriving art colony since the early 20th century, attracting generations of painters, writers, and sculptors.
  • The surrounding Kazimierz Landscape Park boasts one of the highest densities of loess gorges in all of Europe.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Mikołajki

stop · PL

Known as the pearl of Masuria, Mikołajki is a vibrant lakeside hub offering immediate access to the vast pine forests of Puszcza Piska and the scenic, sandy shores of the Great Masurian Lakes.

Stay: €80-160/n lakeside hotel — cheaper guesthouses €40-80/n

Try: Sielawa (fried vendace), Masurian fish soup, Kartacze (meat-filled potato dumplings)

  • Żegluga Mazurska Lake Cruise ~€12-20 · Pre-booking online is highly recommended during the peak summer season to secure preferred time slots.
  • Kadzidłowo Wild Animal Park ~€10 · Located 15 minutes away; entry is by guided tour only, and tickets are purchased on-site.
  • Where to eat
  • Restauracja Sielawa — Lakeside venue serving traditional regional fish dishes, including crispy fried vendace and perch, ~€20-35/pp.
  • Bella Italia Mikołajki — Charming spot offering authentic wood-fired pizzas, fresh seafood pasta, and Italian wines, ~€15-25/pp.
  • Spiżarnia — Cozy restaurant specializing in perfectly grilled local beef steaks and hearty Polish game meats, ~€25-45/pp.
  • In & around
  • Pisz Forest (Puszcza Piska) — 15 min drive, massive pine forest with scenic hiking trails and wild nature
  • Łuknajno Lake Reserve — 10 min drive, UNESCO biosphere reserve featuring observation towers for wild swan watching
  • Krutynia River — 25 min drive, famous for peaceful kayaking routes through lush, shaded woodlands
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in the town center is limited and strictly metered; ensure your accommodation provides dedicated parking.
  • Mosquitoes and midges are highly active near the lakes and in the pine forests; bring strong insect repellent.
  • Many smaller local shops and forest-side stalls accept cash only, so keep Polish Zloty (PLN) on hand.
  • Interesting facts
  • According to local legend, the town is protected by the King of Vendace (Król Sielaw), a mythical giant fish chained to the town's bridge.
  • Mikołajki is named after Saint Nicholas, the patron saint of sailors, reflecting its deep-rooted maritime and fishing history.
  • It serves as a key harbor on the Great Masurian Lakes trail, a continuous 111-kilometer navigable waterway system.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Sopot

stop · PL

Poland's premier seaside resort, Sopot blends elegant Belle Époque villas and a vibrant pier with coastal pine forests and a thriving art scene.

Stay: €90-160/night beachfront/center — cheaper near Gdańsk border €60-90/night

Try: Sopot herring (Śledź po sopocku), smoked Baltic salmon, pierogi with seafood

  • Sopot Pier (Molo w Sopocie) ~€2.50 · Tickets required from late April to late September; free entry in winter.
  • State Art Gallery Sopot ~€4 · Located right next to the pier, showcasing modern Polish art.
  • Where to eat
  • Ristorante Tesoro — Authentic Neapolitan pizzas and fresh seafood pasta in a cozy, bustling atmosphere, ~€15-25/pp.
  • Tawerna Rybna — Freshly caught Baltic cod and halibut served in a rustic seaside setting, ~€20-30/pp.
  • L'Entre Villes — Premium Polish and Argentinian steaks served in a restored 100-year-old villa, ~€40-60/pp.
  • In & around
  • Tri-City Landscape Park — 10 min drive, extensive hiking trails through dense pine and beech forests.
  • Gdynia Orłowo Cliff — 15 min drive, scenic coastal hike along dramatic clay cliffs and quiet sandy beaches.
  • Gdańsk Old Town — 25 min drive, historic Hanseatic architecture, museums, and amber galleries.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in central Sopot is highly restricted and expensive; look for accommodation with private parking included.
  • Monciak (Monte Cassino street) gets extremely crowded on summer evenings; head to the forest paths for peace.
  • The pier charges entry fees only during the high season (April-September); off-season access is free.
  • Interesting facts
  • Sopot boasts the longest wooden pier in Europe, stretching over 511 meters into the Bay of Gdańsk.
  • The Forest Opera (Opera Leśna) is a covered open-air amphitheater hidden in the forest, famous for its incredible acoustics.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Ustka

stop · PL

Ustka is a historic Baltic spa town where wide, powdery sandy beaches meet fragrant coastal pine forests, offering scenic cliffside hiking and a vibrant seaside promenade.

Stay: €70-130/n near the beach — cheaper in the pine forest outskirts €45-80/n

Try: Baltic herring (śledź), smoked flounder (flądra), sea buckthorn liqueur

  • Słowiński National Park ~€2 · Ticket required to access the famous shifting dunes and forest trails.
  • Ustka Lighthouse ~€3 · Pay at the entrance; offers panoramic views of the harbor and beach.
  • Bread Museum in Ustka ~€3 · Cash only; features historical baking equipment and local folklore.
  • Where to eat
  • Syrenka — Renowned local tavern serving traditional Baltic cod in butter sauce and rich fish soup (~€15-25/pp).
  • Dym Na Wodzie — Upscale dining featuring dry-aged steaks, local game, and creative Baltic seafood dishes (~€25-45/pp).
  • La Strada — Cozy Italian spot offering authentic wood-fired pizzas and fresh seafood pasta (~€12-22/pp).
  • In & around
  • Słowiński National Park — 45 min drive, home to massive shifting sand dunes and coastal pine forests.
  • Dolina Charlotty — 15 min drive, a peaceful valley featuring a lake, forest trails, and a seal sanctuary.
  • Rowokół Hill — 35 min drive, a holy Slavic mountain with an observation tower overlooking lakes and forests.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in the town center and near the beach is paid (SPP zone) and heavily monitored during the summer season.
  • To escape the crowds, cross the Słupia River to the Western Beach (Plaża Zachodnia) for quiet pine-forest hiking trails.
  • Carry some Polish Złoty (PLN) cash, as smaller beachside fish fry stands and ice cream kiosks may not accept cards.
  • Interesting facts
  • Ustka is divided into two parts by the Słupia River, connected by a unique pedestrian swing bridge that opens every hour.
  • The town holds official health resort status due to its therapeutic microclimate, mineral springs, and rich peat deposits.
  • The local pine forests release natural essential oils that mix with sea iodine, creating a therapeutic aerosol along the coast.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Kołobrzeg

stop · PL

A historic Baltic spa town where wide sandy beaches meet dense coastal pine forests, offering a mix of maritime history, art, and fresh seafood.

Stay: €70-130/n seaside spa district — cheaper in city center €50-90/n

Try: Smażona flądra (pan-fried flounder), Kołobrzeski ogórek kiszony (locally brined cucumber), smoked Baltic eel

  • Kołobrzeg Lighthouse ~€2.50 · Pay at the entrance; cash is preferred for quick entry to the panoramic viewing platform.
  • Museum of Polish Arms ~€5.00 · Includes access to the outdoor military exhibition featuring tanks and aircraft.
  • Where to eat
  • Restauracja Sublima — Upscale dining featuring excellent dry-aged steaks, modern European dishes, and an extensive wine list, ~€30-45/pp.
  • Restauracja Barka — A local favorite for fresh Baltic seafood, serving traditional fish soup and perfectly fried cod, ~€15-25/pp.
  • Trattoria Venezia — Authentic Italian restaurant offering wood-fired pizzas, fresh homemade pasta, and classic tiramisu, ~€15-25/pp.
  • In & around
  • Ekopark Wschodni — 10 min drive, scenic wooden boardwalks through coastal swamps, peat bogs, and pine forests.
  • Trzęsacz Ruins — 45 min drive, dramatic ruins of a 15th-century church perched on a collapsing cliff edge.
  • Hortulus Spectabilis Gardens in Dobrzyca — 25 min drive, massive theme gardens featuring the world's largest hornbeam maze.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Paid parking zones (SPP) apply strictly throughout the city center and spa district; use mobile apps like EasyPark to manage payments.
  • The coastal cycle paths are highly active; stay alert and avoid walking in designated bike lanes along the promenade.
  • Summer crowds pack the central beach; head west towards Grzybowo for quieter, wider sandy stretches bordered by pine trees.
  • Interesting facts
  • Kołobrzeg is Poland's largest spa town, renowned for its natural brine springs and therapeutic peat (borowina) deposits.
  • The city was declared a fortress during WWII and was over 90% destroyed in 1945, meaning most 'historic' buildings are meticulous post-war reconstructions.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Międzyzdroje

stop · PL

Nestled between the dramatic cliffs of Wolin National Park and a wide Baltic beach, Międzyzdroje offers a classic seaside promenade, dense pine forests, and a unique bison sanctuary.

Stay: €70-120/n beachfront hotels — cheaper guesthouses inland €40-70/n

Try: Smażona flądra (pan-fried flounder), Wędzany dorsz (smoked cod), Gofry z jagodami (waffles with wild blueberries)

  • Wolin National Park Bison Showcase Farm ~€4 · Tickets purchased at the gate; closed on Mondays.
  • Baltic Miniature Park ~€10 · Features 1:25 scale models of Baltic lighthouses and monuments.
  • Where to eat
  • Restauracja Carmen — Wood-fired Italian pizzas, fresh pasta, and high-quality steaks in a cozy setting, ~€20-35/pp.
  • Smażalnia Ryb Port — Traditional Baltic fish joint serving perfectly fried cod, halibut, and flounder, ~€15-25/pp.
  • In & around
  • Świnoujście — 25 min drive, featuring the historic Gerhard's Fort and the tallest lighthouse on the Baltic Sea
  • Turquoise Lake in Wapnica — 10 min drive, a vibrant green-blue flooded former chalk quarry surrounded by hills
  • Kawcza Góra Viewpoint — 5 min drive, offering a dramatic cliffside view and steep wooden stairs leading down to the beach
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in the town center is strictly paid (SPP zone) and heavily enforced; secure a spot at your lodging.
  • The Promenade of Stars gets incredibly crowded during summer afternoons; walk early in the morning for a peaceful experience.
  • Mosquitoes are highly active in the dense pine forests of Wolin National Park; bring strong insect repellent for hikes.
  • Interesting facts
  • The town's Promenade of Stars features over a hundred bronze handprints of famous Polish actors and filmmakers.
  • Wolin Island, where Międzyzdroje is located, is Poland's largest island and was once a notorious Viking stronghold.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details

More photos on Google Images →

Janské Lázně

stop · CZ

A historic spa town nestled in the dense pine forests of the Giant Mountains, offering a perfect blend of thermal relaxation, scenic mountain hiking, and Art Nouveau charm.

Stay: €70-130/n near the Colonnade — cheaper guesthouses on the outskirts €50-80/n

Try: Krkonošské kyselo (sour sourdough soup), Borůvkové knedlíky (blueberry dumplings), Janské Lázně spa wafers

  • Stezka korunami stromů Krkonoše (Treetop Walkway) ~€13 · Buy online to skip the ticket booth queue during sunny weekends.
  • Černohorský Express Cable Car ~€11 · Discounted combo tickets with the treetop walk are available at the base station.
  • Where to eat
  • Restaurace Lesní dům — Juicy beef steaks, wild game specialties, and fresh trout served in a cozy, rustic pine-wood interior. ~€18-30/pp
  • Pizzeria u Šmuthů — Authentic wood-fired Italian pizzas, fresh pasta, and local beers in a relaxed family atmosphere. ~€12-20/pp
  • In & around
  • Adršpach-Teplice Rocks — 45 min drive, stunning sandstone towers, deep pine forests, and a crystal-clear lake with a sandy beach.
  • Sněžka Mountain (Pec pod Sněžkou) — 20 min drive, hike or ride the cable car to the highest peak in the Czech Republic.
  • Vrchlabí Castle & Krkonoše Museum — 20 min drive, interactive exhibits on local mountain history, ecology, and regional art.
  • Notes — read before you go
  • Parking in the town center is strictly regulated; use the large central parking lot near the cable car station.
  • A digital Czech highway vignette is required if your road trip route uses major motorways like the D11.
  • Pack warm layers and sturdy hiking boots, as mountain weather on Černá hora can change rapidly even in mid-summer.
  • Interesting facts
  • It is the only spa town on the Czech side of the Giant Mountains, historically renowned for treating polio and musculoskeletal disorders.
  • The local thermal springs were reportedly discovered in the year 1006 by a squire named Jan of Chockov.
  • The town's elegant Art Nouveau Colonnade, built in 1904, remains the cultural heart of the resort.

⭐ must-see · 🏖 beach · 🍴 where to eat · sight — click a pin for details
